message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infections and hospitalizations surged sharply in 2022 among young children. To assess whether COVID-19 contributed to this surge, we leveraged a real-time nation-wide US database of electronic health records (EHRs) using time series analysis from January 1, 2010 through January 31, 2023, and propensity-score matched cohort comparisons for children aged 0
                  <jats:bold>–</jats:bold>
                  5 years with or without prior COVID-19 infection. Seasonal patterns of medically attended RSV infections were significantly disrupted during the COVID-19 pandemic. The monthly incidence rate for first-time medically attended cases, most of which were severe RSV-associated diseases, reached a historical high rate of 2,182 cases per 1,0000,000 person-days in November 2022, corresponding to a related increase of 143% compared to expected peak rate (rate ratio: 2.43, 95% CI: 2.25
                  <jats:bold>–</jats:bold>
                  2.63). Among 228,940 children aged 0
                  <jats:bold>–</jats:bold>
                  5 years, the risk for first-time medically attended RSV during 10/2022
                  <jats:bold>–</jats:bold>
                  12/2022 was 6.40% for children with prior COVID-19 infection, higher than 4.30% for the matched children without COVID-19 (risk ratio or RR: 1.40, 95% CI: 1.27
                  <jats:bold>–</jats:bold>
                  1.55); and among 99,105 children aged 0
                  <jats:bold>–</jats:bold>
                  1 year, the overall risk was 7.90% for those with prior COVID-19 infection, higher than 5.64% for matched children without (RR: 1.40, 95% CI: 1.21
                  <jats:bold>–</jats:bold>
                  1.62). These data provide evidence that COVID-19 contributed to the 2022 surge of severe pediatric RSV cases.
                </jats:p>
title: Disrupted seasonality and association of COVID-19 with medically attended respiratory syncytial virus infections among young children in the US: January 2010–January 2023

reference.article-title: Quantifying the RSV immunity debt following COVID-19: a public health matter
